question: why do living things need soil
title: Why Do Living Things Need Soil? Because Soil Is the Foundation of All Life on Land
metaDescription: Soil isn't just a growing medium. It's the foundation of the entire terrestrial food web. Here's why living things — including you — depend on healthy soil.
content:
# Why Do Living Things Need Soil? Because Soil Is the Foundation of All Life on Land

Soil is easy to take for granted. You walk on it. You dig in it. You track it into the house and sweep it up. It just seems like the stuff the world is sitting on. But here's the truth: without living soil, there is no food. There are no forests. There are no grasslands. There is no terrestrial life of any kind beyond what can cling to bare rock.

Soil is not the background of life. Soil is the engine of life. And understanding why that's true — not just in a vague conceptual sense but in a specific, biological, chain-of-events sense — is one of the most important things any person who eats food can come to understand.

## What Soil Actually Does

Let's start with the basics. Most terrestrial plants can't grow in bare mineral material — in crushed rock or pure sand. They need something more. They need soil.

Soil is what happens when mineral material — rock, sand, clay — gets colonized by living organisms over time and those organisms begin their multigenerational work of breaking things down and building things up. Bacteria arrive first, processing minerals and producing organic acids that break rock down further. Fungi follow, threading their networks through the mineral matrix, mining trace elements. Dead bacteria and fungi accumulate and become organic matter. Larger organisms arrive to eat the bacteria and fungi. Their waste adds to the organic matter. Over decades and centuries, a true soil ecosystem develops — a living community with structure, chemistry, and biology all working together.

That living community does several things that nothing else can replicate. It stores water and releases it slowly. It cycles nutrients — taking locked-up nitrogen, phosphorus, potassium, and dozens of trace minerals and converting them into forms that plant roots can absorb. It builds physical structure that allows roots to penetrate, water to drain, and air to circulate. And it connects plants to each other and to the mineral world through fungal networks of stunning complexity.

Without these functions, plants die. Without plants, animals have nothing to eat. The whole terrestrial food web — from the insects to the birds to the large mammals to the humans at the top — runs on energy that was originally captured by plants growing in living soil.

## The Soil Food Web Explained

A concept worth understanding deeply is what ecologists call the soil food web. It's a real structure — a community of organisms organized by who eats whom, cycling energy and nutrients through the soil ecosystem.

At the base are the primary decomposers: bacteria and fungi. Bacteria break down simple organic compounds — sugars, proteins, simple starches. Fungi handle the harder stuff — lignin, cellulose, the structural materials in wood and straw that bacteria can't efficiently process. Together, they convert dead organic matter back into its component parts.

Above them are the primary consumers of bacteria and fungi: protozoa, nematodes, and small arthropods. When a protozoan eats a bacterium, it releases the nutrients locked in that bacterial cell back into the soil solution in forms that plant roots can absorb. Every time anything eats anything else in this food web, more nutrients become plant-available. This is the mechanism of soil fertility.

Earthworms operate at a higher level of this system, consuming both organic matter and microbial biomass and producing casts — a material so nutrient-rich that it represents some of the most productive growing medium on earth. A soil full of earthworms is a soil where the food web is functioning from top to bottom.

And plants are not passive recipients of this system. They're active participants. Plant roots exude carbon compounds — sugars, amino acids — into the surrounding soil to attract and feed specific microorganisms. In the rhizosphere, the zone of soil immediately around roots, microbial activity is orders of magnitude higher than in the bulk soil. Plants are essentially farming their own microbiome. A healthy plant in healthy soil has a functioning biological support system of extraordinary complexity.

## Albert Howard's Insight

Albert Howard documented this with rigorous care in the early 20th century. His work at Indore in India — developing what became known as the Indore composting method — was built on a fundamental insight: the decay cycle is the foundation of agricultural fertility.

What goes in as organic matter — plant residues, animal manures, food scraps — gets broken down by the soil food web and released as available nutrients. When that cycle runs intact, the land is self-renewing. Plants grow, they die, they feed the soil, the soil feeds the next generation of plants. The cycle runs without external input.

When the cycle is interrupted — by removing organic matter from the land, by killing the organisms in the soil, by substituting synthetic chemistry for biological process — the land begins to degrade. Howard saw this contrast between Indian farmers who maintained the cycle and British-influenced farms that were already showing the signs of biological depletion that would become catastrophic in the 20th century.

His book *The Soil and Health* is as relevant today as when it was written. The birthright of plants — his term — is health. And that health comes from living soil.

## What Happens When Soil Dies

We have a working example of what happens when soil health degrades at scale. It's the story of modern industrial agriculture over the last 70 years.

Soil organic matter content has declined on cultivated land across much of the world. Tillage, synthetic fertilizers, and monoculture cropping have systematically reduced the biological activity in agricultural soil. The short-term fix — more synthetic inputs — has kept crop yields high in the near term while the underlying soil resource has continued to degrade.

Gabe Brown, in *Dirt to Soil*, describes the conditions he inherited on his North Dakota farm and the decades-long process of rebuilding them by returning to the principles Howard identified a century earlier: cover crops, no-till, diverse rotations, compost, and keeping living roots in the soil year-round. The soil biology came back. The earthworm populations returned. The water infiltration improved. The need for external inputs declined.

This is proof of concept at scale. Living soil is not just nice to have. It's the foundation of sustainable food production. And rebuilding it is possible.

## Why Every Person Needs to Understand This

A hundred years ago, most families in America had a direct relationship with food production. They farmed, or they knew farmers, or they kept a kitchen garden. The connection between soil health and food quality was a lived reality, not an abstraction.

That connection has been severed for most of us. The food comes from somewhere else, grown by someone else, in soil we've never seen. We've outsourced the most fundamental biological relationship in human life — our connection to the soil that feeds us — to industrial systems that prioritize yield and cost over biology and nutrition.

More of us growing food — even backyard gardens, even a few raised beds — is one part of fixing this. When you grow food in living soil, you understand in a visceral way what soil does. You see the earthworms. You smell the compost. You watch plants thrive in biologically active ground in a way they never do in depleted dirt.

Food and water sufficiency at the community level — not just in isolated homesteads but in the aggregate, with houses and cities having productive growing space — is the first step toward real independence. It's also the first step toward real nutritional quality. The soil is where that starts.

- How Healthy Soil Makes Healthy Plants and Ecosystems — The Conservation Foundation
- The Soil Food Web and Nutrient Cycling — AHDB
- Understanding the Soil Food Web — joegardener
- Soil Food Web — Wikipedia
